title: FSet: Functional Sets, Maps, and Sequences

sections.markdown:
# FSet: Functional Sets, Maps, and Sequences

FSet brings persistent (immutable) collections to Common Lisp: every "update" returns a new collection that shares structure with the old. Think Clojure's data structures, but with Lisp-2 ergonomics.

Three workhorses:

* `(fset:set ELEM ...)` — an unordered set with O(log n) membership/insert/delete.
* `(fset:map (KEY VALUE) ...)` — a key-value map, similarly persistent.
* `(fset:seq ELEM ...)` — a persistent sequence.

Core operations are uniform across the three:

* `(fset:with COLL ITEM-OR-KEY [VALUE])` — return a copy with the item/binding added.
* `(fset:less COLL ITEM-OR-KEY)` — return a copy with it removed.
* `(fset:lookup MAP KEY)` — get the bound value (returns two values: value, present-p).
* `(fset:size COLL)` — element count.
* `(fset:union S1 S2)`, `(fset:intersection S1 S2)`, `(fset:set-difference S1 S2)`.
* `(fset:contains? COLL ITEM)` — membership test.

FSet maps are not `eq`-keyed; they're `compare`-keyed (using FSet's generic compare protocol). Numbers, strings, symbols, conses all work; user-defined classes need a `compare` method.

sections.explain: Duplicates are dropped — it's a set.
WITH returns a NEW set with 4 added; the original is unchanged.
Build an empty map, add :NAME -> "ada", look it up.

sections.prompt: Define COUNT-DISTINCT (LIST) returning the number of distinct elements in LIST using FSet. Build a set by folding WITH over an empty FSet:SET, then return its SIZE.
